name: The Pulley
tagline: A grooved wheel redirecting rope tension to lift, pull, and build mechanical advantage systems
problem:
Lifting stone for construction, drawing water from wells, and tensioning ship rigging all require applying large forces along directions human posture cannot sustain. Rope alone cuts hands and wastes friction over edges; sharp edges abrade fibres. The pulley problem is providing a low-friction curved support that preserves rope integrity while changing force direction — and, in block-and-tackle systems, multiplying force at the expense of rope travel.

Pulleys also appear in power transmission: leather belts on stepped cone pulleys change speed ratios between shafts before geared heads dominate machine tools.

Misunderstanding pulleys leads to dangerous false security — mechanical advantage never removes the need to anchor frames against reaction forces.
overview:
A fixed pulley redirects tension ideally without changing magnitude (neglecting friction). A movable pulley attached to the load halves the required pull force in ideal 1:2 systems because two rope segments support the load. Compounding with multiple sheaves creates tackle rove to trade rope pull distance for force.

Real sheaves have bearing friction, rope bending hysteresis, and efficiency losses that accumulate in long tackles. Sheave diameter should be large relative to rope diameter to preserve rope strength — small diameters fatigue fibres.

Materials progress from grooved wood discs on wooden pins, to bronze bearings, to ball-bearing sheaves in modern lifting. Frames (blocks) must be closed shapes that cannot spit the rope under side loading.

Alignment matters: fleet angles on multi-wrap drums must stay within manufacturer limits — historical analogues still fail if rope piles on one flange.

principles.name: Tension continuity (ideal)
principles.explanation: Along a continuous rope strand on massless frictionless sheaves, tension is uniform — compounding alters mechanical advantage by segment counting.

principles.name: Capstan equation
principles.explanation: Friction on wrapped drums grows exponentially with wrap angle — why a few turns on a capstan holds enormous load with modest holding force.

principles.name: Bending fatigue
principles.explanation: Each bend cycles outer and inner fibres — undersized sheaves shorten rope life via internal heating and broken wires/strands.

principles.name: Reaction forces on frames
principles.explanation: Block cheeks see resultant of rope tensions; misalignment induces bending that cracks wooden shells or bends iron straps.

components.name: Sheave (pulley wheel)
components.function: Supports and guides rope with minimal wear
components.madeFrom: Lignum vitae historically, cast iron later, aluminium in aircraft
components.criticalNote: Groove radius matches rope — V-grooves crush soft ropes

components.name: Axle pin / bearing
components.function: Carries radial load while allowing rotation
components.madeFrom: Hardened steel pin with bronze bushing or rolling bearing
components.criticalNote: Lubrication ports must not admit grit

components.name: Block shell (cheeks)
components.madeFrom: Wood strapped with iron, later cast or forged steel
components.criticalNote: Open hooks are outlawed in modern lifting — use closed shackle paths

components.name: Becket anchor point
components.function: Terminates one rope end in tackle systems
components.criticalNote: Sharp bends at termination cut strength — use thimbles

rawMaterials.name: Rope
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Splice eyes with full strength methods — knots derate

rawMaterials.name: Lubricant
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Match lubricant to load and temperature

buildSteps.title: Size sheave for rope
buildSteps.description: Choose sheave diameter at least ~10× rope diameter for natural fibre; modern wire rope charts stricter. Mark groove profile to match rope crown.
buildSteps.warningNote: Undersized sheaves are a silent overload mechanism

buildSteps.title: Turn sheave and bore
buildSteps.description: Lathe turn or pole lathe equivalent; bore for pin with reamed fit. Balance for high-speed belt use if applicable.
buildSteps.warningNote: Off-centre groove walks rope off edge

buildSteps.title: Fabricate cheeks and assemble
buildSteps.description: Bolt or strap cheeks with correct spacing — rope must not pinch between cheek and sheave edge.
buildSteps.warningNote: Cheek bolts projecting into rope path are cutting hazards

buildSteps.title: Rove tackle and proof test
buildSteps.description: Rig fall line from planned direction; hoist known test mass ≥1.25× working load if possible. Listen for bearing grind.
buildSteps.warningNote: Stay clear of bight — recoil if rope breaks is lethal

buildSteps.title: Mark SWL and inspect schedule
buildSteps.description: Paint or tag safe working load and date. Document bend radius and rope type pairing — future users depend on labels.
buildSteps.warningNote: Never exceed SWL because 'it felt fine last time'

history.event: Compound pulleys appear in Mediterranean mechanical traditions associated with lifting and siege engines.
Factory line-shafting spreads belt-and-stepped-pulley drive systems that distribute power from central steam engines to rows of machines — a signature layout of industrial-era workshops.

inventors.name: Heron of Alexandria
inventors.contribution: Surviving texts describe simple machines including pulley-based mechanical principles taught in antiquity

impact:
Pulleys are force routers. They appear in cranes, elevators, ships, wells, and later in engines as belt drives. Understanding tackles prevents both wasted labour and rigging collapses.

The steam-engine entry treats pulleys as part of the factory transmission stack that made stationary steam economically legible at scale.
